note: load-tester subagent hit repeated stream-idle timeouts; orchestrator ran the ramp directly via authenticated ctx_execute against the live hardened backend.
payload: full valid schema {id,user_id,amount,location,timestamp,merchant}, unique user_id+id per request, amount $10-200 (stays LOW), to avoid velocity-freeze/423 skew
knee_note: No knee/saturation within c<=50. Latency scales ~linearly with concurrency (p50 ~ 0.65ms * c), consistent with single-threaded SQLite serializing under a global lock; per-request work is sub-millisecond. First request shows ~41ms cold-start warmup (p95 at c=1).
findings: Throughput and latency are healthy: 238/238 requests returned 200 with zero errors; steady-state p50 ~1.7ms, p95 <= ~39ms even at concurrency 50; peak ~1300 rps.

RATE LIMITING IS CONFIGURED BUT NOT ENFORCED: 90 simultaneous (and 238 total within the minute) requests all returned 200, zero 429. Root cause in code: slowapi Limiter + @limiter.limit decorator + RateLimitExceeded handler are present, but app.add_middleware(SlowAPIMiddleware) is NEVER called (only CORSMiddleware is added at main.py:56). The decorator is inert without the middleware. This confirms the prior-mission 'no rate limiting on /analyze' finding and contradicts Phase 5's 'PARTIAL/configured' read.

Latency grows linearly with concurrency rather than hitting a sharp knee, indicating request serialization (single global SQLite lock) rather than parallel processing. Fine at demo scale; would cap single-process throughput under heavy real load.
No 5xx, no connection failures, no timeouts at any tested level.

Because the in-process rate limiter is inert, /analyze has NO abuse ceiling other than auth — a valid API key can drive unbounded volume (relevant to the card-testing / enumeration attack surface from Phase 4).
